dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 6 at the redacted address for a geriatric altered mental status call.
Hello, ma'am, I'm Josh, a paramedic with the rescue team. Can you or someone else tell me what brought you to call 911 today?
She fainted earlier and is now conscious but confused. She's not herself since the episode.
Ma'am, what is your name?
Elizabeth.
Grip my finger-squeeze, ma'am. Good. She localizes appropriately when asked to respond to pain.
Eye opening is spontaneous. She acknowledges voice and movement.
Airway is patent and not in distress. No signs of obstruction.
Respiratory rate is 18, even. Work of breathing appears normal with no use of accessory muscles.
Pulse is 93, regular. Blood pressure is 179 over 68. Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Can you tell me what was happening before the episode?
Not to our knowledge. We thought she was fine after her PCP appointment today, but we don't know the time of that visit.
She did eat, but we can't recall when. Prescribed medications appear to be followed.
Are you experiencing any pain, ma'am?
No pain. Just a fainting episode followed by confusion.
Head is normocephalic with no trauma. Trachea is midline. Mentation is intact-she knows our roles and location. No JVD noted.
No drainage. Mucus membranes are pink and moist. Right eye is clouded. No nystagmus or hemoptysis observed.
Chest rises equal with respirations. Breath sounds equal on both sides with no adventitious sounds noted. No trauma observed.
Skin is warm, dry, and pink, except for the fainting episode. Temperature is 101.7 degrees. No rashes or hives present.
Extraocular movement is intact. She localizes appropriately. Sclera is white in the left eye. Conscious and alert, oriented to person and place.
Full range of motion in all four limbs. Strong, regular distal pulses palpated bilaterally.
No slurred speech, facial droop, or unilateral weakness. No numbness or tingling in extremities. No evidence of LOC based on findings.
Abdomen is soft, non-distended. Bowel sounds not reported.
Pelvis appears stable. No pelvic pain or tenderness observed.
Pulse is 88, still regular and within expected limits for her age.
Respiratory rate is 16-within normal limits.
Blood pressure decreased to 170 over 66-still elevated.
Oxygen saturation improved to 99, up from the initial 80 recorded.
Blood glucose level was 174 initially.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm with minimal ST depression for a 40+ female, per protocol.
IV attempt made on the left arm. Slightly difficult and unsuccessful after one attempt.
12-lead ECG acquired-showing borderline findings per standing protocol.
Following Medical Protocol 3-15 for altered mental status with fever.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 14-alert and oriented to person and place, but not to time.
[COMPANY_NAME] confirmed as receiving facility. All emergency alerts have been sent.
We're securing a stairchair for movement from bed to stretcher. Seat belts have been applied.
Five-point harness applied. Patient remains alert during transfer.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the designated destination. Transport confirmed.
No changes in condition during transport. Vital signs remained stable.
[COMPANY_NAME] has been updated with key findings. Diagnosis-altered mental status with fever.
Patient transferred to ED-17. Personal items left in patient room as per policy.
She has a known history of primary hypertension. Hospital has been informed about it.
She is secured on the stretcher with all restraints in place for transport.
Repeat vitals show no changes. Oxygen saturation decreased but stabilized.
No significant neurological changes-she remains oriented and alert.
Blood glucose was re-measured at 174-no hypoglycemic or diabetic symptoms found.
Patient is en route to [COMPANY_NAME] with no issues reported.
All actions completed to safely transport patient. No pain reported throughout assessment and transport.
